content:
4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Native <input type="time"> is one of those HTML elements that seems convenient—until you actually try to use it.
It looks different in every browser, offers almost no styling flexibility, and the desktop experience is inconsistent. If you’re building a design system or a professional web application, it usually isn’t good enough.
In this article, we’ll build a fully accessible Time Picker from scratch using React Aria Components, @internationalized/date, and Tailwind CSS.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The final component includes:

• ⌨️ Keyboard-editable HH:MM:SS segments

• 📜 Scrollable hour, minute, and second columns

• ♿ Full accessibility powered by React Aria

• 🎨 Completely customizable styling

• 🧩 Slot-based design system integration

• 🚀 Portal rendering for popovers

• 🌙 Built-in dark mode support

Final Architecture
Our component is split into several small, reusable pieces.
TimePicker
├── TimePickerInput (trigger)
└── Portal
└── TimePickerPanel
├── TimeSegmentInput
├── TimeColumn (Hour)
├── TimeColumn (Minute)
├── TimeColumn (Second)
└── Clear ButtonInstead of placing all logic inside one component, the state lives inside a custom hook:
useTimePickerState()This hook manages:

• Opening and closing the panel

• Current selected value

• Internal editing state

• Available options

• Committing changes back to the parent
Keeping UI separate from state makes the component much easier to maintain.

Step 1 — Managing Time with @internationalized/date
Internally we store values using the immutable Time object from @internationalized/date.
Consumers of the component still interact with simple strings:
09:30or
09:30:15Parsing
import { Time, parseTime } from "@internationalized/date"
function parseTimeString(value?: string): Time | null {
...
}Formatting
function formatTimeString(time: Time, includeSeconds: boolean) {
...
}This gives us:

• Immutable objects

• Type safety

• Easy manipulation

• Simple external API

Step 2 — The State Hook
The custom hook handles almost everything.
Responsibilities include:

• Opening and closing the popover

• Synchronizing controlled values

• Building hour/minute/second options

• Handling disabled values

• Committing changes

• Managing default values
const {
isOpen,
fieldValue,
panelTime,
columnOptions,
commitTime
} = useTimePickerState(...)Separating this logic keeps every UI component almost stateless.

Step 3 — Editable Time Segments
Instead of using a single input, each time section becomes its own editable field.
09 : 45 : 30
^^ ^^ ^^Each segment supports:

• Typing numbers

• Auto zero-padding

• Arrow Up / Down

• Tab navigation

• Enter to confirm

• Escape to cancel
Example:
<input
inputMode="numeric"
maxLength={2}
value={pad(value)}
onChange={handleChange}
onKeyDown={handleKeyDown}
/>After two digits are entered, the component automatically:

• Validates the value

• Updates state

• Moves focus to the next segment
That creates a surprisingly smooth editing experience.

Step 4 — Scrollable Columns with React Aria
The dropdown consists of three independent ListBox components.
Each column is powered by React Aria:
<ListBox
selectionMode="single"
disallowEmptySelection
selectedKeys={[String(selectedValue)]}
>React Aria automatically provides:

• Keyboard navigation

• Proper ARIA roles

• Screen reader announcements

• Focus management

• Selection behavior
Every item is simply a ListBoxItem.
<ListBoxItem>
{option.label}
</ListBoxItem>When the value changes, we automatically scroll the selected option into view.
selected?.scrollIntoView({
block: "nearest"
})
Step 5 — Styling Without Shipping CSS
Rather than shipping a stylesheet, every visual part is defined with Tailwind classes.
export const defaultStyles = {
panel: "...",
inputWrapper: "...",
columnItemSelected: "...",
}Every style can be overridden:
<TimePicker
classNames={{
panel: "shadow-2xl border-0",
columnItemSelected: "bg-purple-600"
}}
/>Using clsx together with tailwind-merge ensures custom styles override defaults cleanly.

Step 6 — Design System Slots
Different teams use different UI libraries.
Instead of forcing consumers to use our HTML, we expose slots.
<TimePicker
slots={{
Button: MyButton,
Input: MyInput,
Icon: MyIcon
}}
/>Internally:
const InputComp = slots?.Input ?? DefaultInputThis makes the component easy to integrate with:

• shadcn/ui

• Material UI

• Chakra UI

• Internal design systems

Step 7 — Portal Rendering
Dropdowns often get clipped inside:

• Tables

• Modals

• Scroll containers
To avoid this, the panel renders into document.body.
createPortal(
<TimePickerPanel />,
document.body
)The popover position is calculated using the trigger’s bounding rectangle.
const rect = containerRef.current.getBoundingClientRect()The position updates automatically when:

• the page scrolls

• the window resizes

Accessibility
Accessibility was a primary goal.
The component supports:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

React Aria handles much of the heavy lifting, allowing us to focus on component behavior rather than accessibility plumbing.

Installation
npm install @sandylib27/react-aria-timepicker react-aria-components @internationalized/date
Basic Usage
import { TimePicker } from "@sandylib27/react-aria-timepicker"
function App() {
const [time, setTime] = useState("")
return (
<TimePicker
value={time}
onChange={setTime}
/>
)
}
Custom Configuration
Hide seconds:
<TimePicker showSecond={false} />Use 15-minute intervals:
<TimePicker minuteStep={15} />Disable specific hours:
<TimePicker
disabledHours={() => [0,1,2,3,4,5]}
/>
API Overview
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Conclusion
Building an accessible time picker from scratch doesn’t have to be complicated.
By combining:

• React Aria Components for accessibility

• @internationalized/date for immutable time handling

• Tailwind CSS for flexible styling
we end up with a component that’s easy to customize, fully keyboard accessible, and integrates cleanly into any design system.
The complete source code is available on GitHub, and the package can be installed directly from npm.
npm install @sandylib27/react-aria-timepickerIf you’re building your own component library, React Aria provides an excellent foundation for creating accessible components without reinventing the wheel.
